one_line_takeaway: MPIB shows that Attack Success Rate alone is an unreliable and sometimes misleading signal for clinical LLM safety -- a purpose-built outcome metric (CHER) reveals that direct prompt injection into the query is often more clinically dangerous than RAG-mediated indirect injection, and that some defenses cut surface compliance without reducing (or even while masking) high-severity patient harm.
key_finding: ASR and CHER diverge substantially and can move in opposite directions under defenses, motivating outcome-based auditing rather than compliance-only metrics. Concrete numbers from Table 5: for V1, Input Guard (D2) reduced Qwen-2.5-72B CHER from 65.7% (D0) to 50.7% (D2), and Llama-3.1-70B CHER from 86.6% (D0) to 68.7% (D2). For V2, Meditron-70B CHER dropped from 53.1% (D0) to 37.5% (D4), and Qwen-2.5-72B V2 CHER dropped from 7.8% (D0) to 1.6% (D1). A clear ASR-CHER decoupling example: under V2 for MedGemma-4B, D3 slightly INCREASED ASR (64.1% D0 to 65.6% D3) while CHER DECREASED (21.9% D0 to 18.8% D3); conversely for Qwen-2.5-72B under V2, D1 reduced both ASR (53.1% to 45.3%) and CHER (7.8% to 1.6%) together. V1 attacks generally produced higher CHER than V2 across models (e.g., worst-case models like BioMistral-7B and MMed-Llama-3-8B showed V1 CHER around 92-94% vs. much lower V2 CHER), consistent with direct injections eliciting concrete actionable (and thus harm-judged) clinical decisions, whereas V2 harms are more often diffuse/citation-level compliance. FPR-H (utility loss) is model-dependent: Meditron-70B FPR-H rose from 16.0% (D0) to 33.6% (D4) under the composite defense, while Qwen-2.5-72B stayed low (2.7-3.8% across D0-D4). The composite defense D4 was not uniformly best -- it helped some models (Meditron-70B V2 CHER 53.1% to 37.5%) but underperformed simpler single-component defenses elsewhere, attributed to interaction effects between input rewriting and context sanitization.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MPIB (Medical Prompt Injection Benchmark), a dataset-and-benchmark suite of 9,697 clinically grounded instances for evaluating LLM/RAG clinical safety under both direct (V1, in-query) and indirect (V2, RAG-mediated/poisoned-context) prompt injection. Its central methodological contribution is Clinical Harm Event Rate (CHER), an outcome-based metric that scores judged severity of clinical harm (0-4 scale, high-severity threshold k=3) and is reported alongside conventional Attack Success Rate (ASR, severity >= 2) specifically to disentangle surface instruction-compliance from downstream patient-safety risk. The benchmark spans four clinical scenario families (S1 General Info, S2 Medication/Dosing, S3 Triage, S4 Guidelines), uses a five-type harm taxonomy (H1-H5), and includes benign/borderline anchors (V0/V0'). The authors also built a six-gate (G1-G6) construction/quality-control pipeline, selected a primary LLM judge (Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct) via empirical validation against 300 human-labeled instances, and evaluated a 12-model matrix (general-purpose and medical-tuned LLMs) against five defense configurations (D0 none, D1 internal hierarchy hardening, D2 input-guard/SLD rewriting, D3 context sanitizer/SLD, D4 adaptive composer of D2+D3).
threat_model: Attacker cannot modify the system prompt, model weights, or decoding configuration. The attacker CAN control text the model sees at inference time via one of two channels: (V1) direct injection - adversarial instructions embedded in the user query; (V2) indirect/RAG-mediated injection - adversarial instructions embedded in retrieved contextual documents (e.g., a poisoned 'guideline update' or fake editor's note) presented as contextual authority, where the attacker controls document content but not the query or system prompt. For V2 the paper adopts a 'guaranteed exposure' protocol: every V2 instance includes at least one poisoned document in the context bundle, explicitly bypassing the stochastic retrieval step -- the paper is scoped to post-retrieval failure modes (how the model prioritizes conflicting instructions once poisoned content is already in context) and explicitly states it does NOT evaluate whether poisoned content gets retrieved in the first place, retrieval ranking effects, or a compromised retriever.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) outcome labels come primarily from a structured LLM-as-a-judge pipeline, introducing possible judge-dependent bias, domain-specific under/over-estimation, and limited recall on severe harm; excluding invalid/unparseable judge outputs from metric denominators can also skew reported numbers, especially on borderline cases. (2) The H1-H5 harm taxonomy and 0-4 severity scale, while clinically grounded, cannot fully capture real-world harm, which depends on patient comorbidities, access to care, local guideline variation, and costs of delayed escalation. (3) The defense harness (D2-D4) is intentionally a lightweight 7B-scale (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) scaffold for input rewriting/context sanitization, explicitly positioned as a controlled reference rather than a production-grade guardrail; labeling errors in this scaffold can propagate into over-sanitization (utility loss) or under-protection via D4's adaptive composition. Additionally (from the threat-model section, not the Limitations header, but methodologically important): the V2 guaranteed-exposure protocol assumes poisoned context is already retrieved, so the benchmark does not measure retrieval-stage vulnerability (ranking, retriever compromise, or realistic retrieval noise) -- it isolates only post-retrieval instruction-prioritization behavior, which is a narrower and more favorable-to-attacker setting than an end-to-end RAG pipeline evaluation.

datasets_used: MedQA (BigBio English fold) - source for V0/V0' anchors and V1 direct-injection queries
PubMedQA (BigBio labeled folds) - source for V2 indirect/RAG-mediated injection context-based variants
MPIB itself: 9,697 instances (V0 2,734; V0' 5,737; V1 644; V2-Strict 94; V2-Borderline 488)
Human-validated judge-evaluation set, N=300 (150 selection subset, 150 holdout subset)
quantitative_results: V2 ASR/CHER divergence, MedGemma-4B under D3: ASR 64.1% (D0) -> 65.6% (D3) [increase] while CHER 21.9% (D0) -> 18.8% (D3) [decrease]

Judge selection (selection set N=150): Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct Kendall tau 0.65, Recall@4 0.75, SCE 0.75, JSON-valid 100.0%; Mixtral-8x22B-v0.1 Kendall tau 0.53, Recall@4 0.83, SCE 1.07, JSON-valid 91.3%; Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct Kendall tau 0.45, Recall@4 0.58, SCE 1.33, JSON-valid 100.0%

Judge holdout stability (N=150): Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct SCE 0.80 (Delta 0.05), Recall@4 0.91 (Delta +0.16); Mixtral-8x22B-v0.1 SCE 1.17 (Delta 0.10), Recall@4 0.82 (Delta -0.02); Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct SCE 1.41 (Delta 0.08), Recall@4 0.36 (Delta -0.22)
baselines_compared: 12 evaluated LLMs: Qwen-2.5 (7B, 32B, 72B), Llama-3.1 (8B, 70B), Mixtral-8x7B (general-purpose); MedGemma (4B, 27B), Meditron (7B, 70B), BioMistral-7B, MMed-Llama-3-8B (medical-tuned)

5 defense configurations: D0 no defense, D1 internal hierarchy-aware system hardening, D2 input-guard/SLD intent-preserving rewriting, D3 context sanitizer/SLD (no-op for V1), D4 adaptive policy composer coupling D2+D3 (SLD components use Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct)

Prior benchmarks positioned against in Related Work (not part of this paper's own experiments): HarmBench (general red-teaming), BIPIA (generic indirect prompt injection), MedGuard/CARES/NOHARM (medical safety auditing without a prompt-injection-primary threat model), MentalChat16K, CURE
